reasoning: The baseline threshold is the 30-day average of protests over the 360 days before Oct 26, 2025. This reference period is heavily skewed by the massive 'vie chère' unrest in Martinique that began in early September 2024 (shifted in backtesting to 2025) and intensified in October. Because this unrest represents a massive spike in activity compared to the rest of the year, the 30-day window covering October (Period 1) is mathematically guaranteed to be the maximum 30-day period of the year, thus overwhelmingly exceeding the 12-month average. I assign Period 1 an 85% probability. For Period 2, covering late Oct to late Nov, the primary group RPPRAC rejected an Oct 24th government protocol and announced a 'seconde phase' of strikes, making it highly probable that protests continue at a high enough rate to beat the ~4-protest threshold (62%). For Period 3 (Late Dec to Jan), Caribbean holiday seasons typically dampen sustained social movements. The historical average will likely remain elevated from the fall peak, making it harder for routine background protests to surpass it once the main movement concludes, so I drop this to 45%, moving closer to 0.5 due to horizon uncertainty.
